danger_signals.visual_form: Animated enemy sprites, varied designs per area
danger_signals.meaning: Direct combat threat; cyborg and Metal Command forces

danger_signals.visual_form: Bullets and energy attacks fired by enemies
danger_signals.meaning: Ranged threat; player can intercept with fists

danger_signals.visual_form: Area completion leads to boss encounter
Player life counter decreases on HUD
danger_signals.meaning: Accumulating damage; proximity to death state

danger_signals.visual_form: Cybernetic arms as protagonist replacement limbs
danger_signals.meaning: World is hostile enough to maim police; military renegades active

safety_signals.visual_form: Gold coins dropped from defeated enemies and containers
safety_signals.meaning: Currency for healing platforms and upgrades

safety_signals.visual_form: Floating robot ally alongside player after collecting letter combos
Player character visually changes when combining with robot (repeated letter collection)
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invincibility and enhanced attacks; safe period

safety_signals.visual_form: Purchasable via platform (2000 coins) or found scattered

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy sprite variations tied to each of seven areas (Area A factory, Area B-F variable, Area G finale); regional Solbrain version features different enemy appearances
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Industrial and militaristic level design; factory (Area A), submarine (Area C in Shatterhand), futuristic facilities (Area G); represents General Gus Grover's cyborg army infrastructure
Police / law enforcement authority; protagonist Steve Hermann is NYPD officer given cyborg augmentation; implicit in game's heroic framing

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Tokusatsu superhero aesthetic; protagonist Daiki Nishio in Solbrain version
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Graphical reskins of enemies and bosses; boss repositioning between areas; carnival theme replaces submarine
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Famicom regional variant reflects TV series storyline; different character graphics, items, and level theming (carnival in Area C vs industrial submarine)

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Year 2030 dystopia with active cyborg militarization
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Futuristic industrial environments; mechanized enemy designs; cybernetic player character arms; multi-stage distributed military infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Metal Command forces actively occupying and defending facilities across seven distinct areas; protagonist in active combat deployment

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Police officer loses arms in Metal Command skirmish
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Protagonist appears with replacement cybernetic arms throughout gameplay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Arms function as primary weapon system; player uses fists as melee and projectile-interception tool

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Seven sequential stage areas with increasing complexity; boss encounters represent command hierarchy
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Areas A-F accessible in any order (distributed threat across regions); Area G unlocks only after all regions cleared (final stronghold)

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Technological parity between law enforcement and renegades
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player robot companions (eight variants) provide technological edge; upgrade system via coin currency
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can swap between eight different robotic satellite designs based on letter collection strategy; temporary fusion form grants enhanced power

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Carnival theme replaces submarine in Area C; futuristic makeover to Area G; graphical reskins of characters and bosses
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss repositioning: Area C boss moves to Area B, Area B boss moves to Area C with modified AI; Solbrain version extends transformation duration (68 seconds vs shorter in Shatterhand)

environmental_storytelling.technique: Level theme progression reflecting threat geography
environmental_storytelling.example: Area A (factory) establishes industrial infrastructure; Areas B-F represent distributed military installations; Area G is fortified final command center with futuristic missile aesthetics

Shatterhand version maintains industrial/military aesthetic throughout; Solbrain (TV-licensed) version introduces carnival in Area C, suggesting different cultural context or civilian areas affected by conflict
Player's replacement arms visually symbolize both the threat (cybernetic militarization) and the response (law enforcement technological parity)

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss character positioning and graphical variance
environmental_storytelling.example: Regional differences in boss designs (Solbrain vs Shatterhand) suggest different commanders in the same conflict; Area B and C boss swap indicates strategic redeployment
Player character changes appearance when fused with robot satellite, providing visual confirmation of power escalation and temporary tactical superiority

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Active (player has collected matching letter trio)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Float alongside player; provide specialized attacks (laser beams for ααβ, sword for αβα, etc.); can be damaged and destroyed by enemies

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Fusion triggered (same letter combination collected twice consecutively)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Merge with player character; grant enhanced attack power and temporary invincibility; visual appearance changes; reverts after duration expires
Varied per area; some fire projectiles, some pursue melee range; different HP values between regional versions (Japan vs US)
Complex AI patterns; repositioned between regional versions; some feature multi-form transformations (e.g., worm-like second form in original Shatterhand Area B boss, removed in Solbrain)
Can hover into air by crouching and holding A button using satellite support; combined offensive capability

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Without robot companion or post-transformation revert
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Reliant on standard melee fist attacks; can intercept projectiles; must accumulate coins for platform upgrades

notes: Shatterhand communicates world-state primarily through environmental design (industrial military facilities escalating to command center) and character augmentation (cybernetic arms as both metaphor and mechanic). The game's regional dual-release (Shatterhand in US/EU, Solbrain in Japan) creates two distinct visual narratives for the same mechanical framework—the industrial/military version vs. the tokusatsu superhero variant. Danger is signaled through enemy density, projectile patterns, and boss encounters; safety through resource acquisition (coins, power-ups) and companion systems (robot satellites). The transformation mechanic provides visual feedback for tactical advantage. Faction identity is implicit rather than explicitly marked; Metal Command is identified through industrial environments and cyborg aesthetics, while L.O.R.D./law enforcement identity comes from protagonist characterization and narrative framing.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Shatterhand article)
